text: We present a fifty seven-year-old female with a large hiatal hernia as confirmed on an upper GI study.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer energy device to take down the short gastric vessels at the greater curvature of the stomach, medializing the stomach and detaching posterior gastric attachments. The approach continues towards the left crus of the diaphragm, entering the hernia sac.

The surgeon begins dissection along the lesser curvature of the stomach, identifying the right cruce of the diaphragm and performing blunt dissection to separate the hernia sac from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to continue medially, reaching the extent of the left-sided dissection. The dissection progresses into the mediastinum, further separating tissue layers.

The surgeon identifies the left vagus nerve and continues the mediastinal esophageal dissection, taking down posterior attachments. The right-sided mediastinal dissection proceeds with caution to avoid the pleura and the right vagus nerve, achieving circumferential control of the esophagus while being mindful of the mediastinal aorta.
The surgeon resects the hernia sac, followed by the insertion of an endoscope to insufflate the stomach, confirming the intra-abdominal location of the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon begins the primary posterior cruroplasty by placing interrupted Ethabon sutures at the posterior aspect of the diaphragm. The sutures are tied intercorporeally using robotic assistance, securing the repair.

A six by nine centimeter piece of Alloderm mesh is cut into a keyhole configuration and placed in the abdomen. The mesh is unfurled and positioned behind the esophagus. The keyhole portion is closed with Ethabon sutures, and the mesh is secured using four-point fixation.
